text:
AcubeSAT is an open-source Space biology project
supported by the 3rd cycle of the Fly Your Satellite! pro-
gramme of the ESA Education Office, with a dual ob-
jective; first, it aims to probe changes in gene expression
in eukaryotic cells when exposed to the effects of mi-
crogravity and radiation in Low-Earth Orbit.  Second, it
seeks to introduce a Lab-on-a-Chip (LoC) platform as a
modular and cost-effective method for conducting high-
throughput Space biology research. Beyond the scientific
goals, SpaceDot, the team that is developing AcubeSAT,
as an interdisciplinary Space research teamof the Aristotle
University of Thessaloniki, aspires to promote aerospace
engineering as an accessible field of study within the aca-
demic community in Greece [1].
Throughout the development, the team focused on cre-
ating a custom On-Board Computer subsystem that would
meet the unique needs of the mission, while ensuring re-
liability in the harsh Space environment [2]. This paper
outlines the journey, from the initial design stages to the
rigorous testing of the PCB, highlighting key milestones,
performed tests, and lessons learned along the way.  A
high-level timeline is illustrated in Figure 1.

2.1 History & Background
Designing AcubeSAT’s custom OBC marked one of
the team’s first ventures into embedded systems devel-
opment.  The STM32L4S9ZIT6 Microcontroller Unit
(MCU) from the STM32 series was initially chosen as
the core of the subsystem, primarily due to the team’s fa-
miliarity with the platform and its suitable peripheral set
that satisfied the mission requirements [3]. As reliability
is paramount for a critical subsystem such as OBC, a dual,
cold-redundant MCU setup was selected.
However, due to space constraints and complexity the
cold-redundant architecture was abandoned for a sim-
pler design with a single MCU. Microchip’s  (Atmel)
SAMV71Q21RT [4] was the new chip replacing the old
STM32-based design, because of its low cost, resistance
to radiation effects (as it has a drop-in pin-compatible
radiation-tolerant version available), and similarity in the
feature set with the previous MCU. Since this design
change, the SAMV71Q21RT has continued to serve as the
primary processing unit for the subsystemup to the present
day. This processor is also employed across some other in-
house built subsystems, including the Payload PCB and the
Attitude, Determination and Control Subsystem (ADCS).
By standardizing on the SAMV71Q21RT, decentralized
processing power is achieved tomeet the computational re-
quirements of each subsystem individually, while also re-
ducing the software development overhead and preserving
IAC–24–E2,4,1,x89777

ess, Milan, Italy, 14-18 October 2023.
ed by the International Astronautical Federation with permission.
robustness due to the radiation hardness of the MCU.
At the hardware implementation level, a single PCB,
that follows the LibreCube Board Specification [5], seen
in Figure 3, hosts two distinct subsystems. One resem-
bling a conventional OBC responsible for telecommand
execution, telemetry retrieval, on-board time synchroniza-
tion, in-orbit patching [6], and system-level FDIR [7]. The
second subsystem entails the implementation of an ADCS
for both nominal and downlinking scenarios [6, 8]. The
two subsystems have their own dedicated SAMV71Q21RT
MCU, for a more distributed processing power and reli-
ability. The decision to combine both subsystems onto a
single PCB was driven by space constraints.  From this
point forth, the PCB hosting the two subsystems will be
referred to as the OBC/ADCS board, with each subsystem
referred to as the OBC side and the ADCS side, respect-
ively.
Software-wise, an early decision was taken to extens-
ively usemodern C++17 for the development of the system
firmware [9],  in order to exploit the extensive compile-
time type safety checks [10] provided by the language
standard. The team deliberately eschewed features such as
dynamic memory allocation, multiple inheritance, virtual
functions, and the default C++ Standard Template Library
(STL), preferring to replace it with an embedded-friendly
implementation, namely ETL [11], in order to reduce the
binary size and make memory allocation behaviors more
predictable. As the complex nature of such a control soft-
ware requires multiple tasks that need to run in parallel,
FreeRTOS [12] was selected as the operating system as it
natively supports the ARMCortex-M7 architecture which
is the core of the SAMV71Q21RT MCU [13]. The soft-
ware source files can be found in the project’s open repos-
itories for OBC [14] and ADCS [15].
The system level design philosophy consists of the In-
tegration Model (IM) (flatsat) and the Proto-flight Model
(PFM) (assembled spacecraft) [16]. Every procured sub-
system component initially integrated into the IM. Spe-
cifically for the OBC/ADCS board, the design approach
includes the production of a Development Model (DM),
an Engineering Qualification Model (EQM) and finally a
Flight Model (FM) [16].
2.2 System Architecture
The OBC is responsible for the reliable management of
information flow throughout the entire spacecraft’s system
[3] and it’s built around the hardware specification seen in
Table 1a.
The chosen MCU has a drop-in pin-compatible re-
placement non-tolerant version that is used for develop-
ment purposes, thus reducing production costs for experi-
mentation [17]. Data storage for mission-critical paramet-

ers is supported by 2MB of Magnetoresistive Random
Access Memory (MRAM) [18],  selected for  its inher-
ent immunity to Single-Event Upset (SEU)s [19], while
non-critical data (e.g.  telemetry) are stored in a 4GB
automotive-grade NOT-AND (NAND) Flash [20].

A brief
high-level diagram of the OBC subsystem architecture is
depicted in Figure 2.
Given the satellite’s prolonged exposure to ionizing ra-
diation, safeguarding critical electronics is essential. To
enhance their resilience in these harsh conditions, the team
developed custom in-house LCL circuits. These circuits
were specifically designed to protect the most vital com-
ponents of the OBC, the two memory modules and the
Control Area Network (CAN) transceivers, from Single-
Event Latch-up (SEL).
The functions of the ADCS subsystem serve to control
the spacecraft’s angular velocity within safe limits, point-
ing during payload data transmission, in addition to sun
pointing for maximal power input.  To achieve that,  the
MCU is connected to a series of sensors that aid in the
estimation of the spacecraft’s orientation and angular ve-
locity, driving the required actuators for control [8]. The
main components that constitute the ADCS can be found
in Table 1b.
All  satellite PCBs,  excluding the Payload Subsys-
tem, are connected through a stack configuration using
LibreCube-compatible PC104 connectors [5].  The 104-
pin connectors provide flexibility, ensuring compatibility
with various Open-Source and Commercial of the Shell
(COTS) hardware modules. Communication between the
OBC and ADCS subsystems, as well as with other space-
craft systems, is facilitated through the CAN Flexible Data
Rate (CAN-FD) bus, which operates with a custom signal-
ing protocol [6] that transports messages according to the
ECSS Packet Utilization Standard (PUS) standard [21].
The bus is cold-redundant, ensuring inter-subsystem com-

munication in case of failure. The SatNOGS Communica-

transceiver specified in Table 1a, was selected for compat-

features, including overvoltage andElectrostaticDischarge
(ESD) safeguards [23].
2.3 Manufacturing
The OBC/ADCS PCB was entirely designed using the
KiCad open-source EDA tool [30], with the design files
available in the project’s GitLab repositories [31].  The
PCB features an 8-layer design, including two layers ded-
icated to components and signal routing, three for ground

To enhance the reliability of theOBC/ADCS board, the
team adopted a specific design approach focused on mitig-
ating radiation effects. Protective measures and redundant
circuitry following the identification of the critical systems
have been implemented (asmentioned in Section 2.2). The
team opted to use COTS components, aligning with the
∗https://www.prismaelectronics.eu
IAC–24–E2,4,1,x89777

ess, Milan, Italy, 14-18 October 2023.
ed by the International Astronautical Federation with permission.
New Space industry trend, a decision that also signific-
antly reduced the costs of the in-house OBC/ADCS board
models.
The component selection followed a common pipeline
of creating a database and gathering existing information
online, with respect  to Total Ionizing Dose (TID) and
Single-Event Effect (SEE) radiation immunity levels. The
approach taken was the addition of a radiation design mar-
gin of at least 1.2 (as mentioned in the ECSS policy for
design margins [33]) to account for any uncertainties con-
cerning the simulated values, as well as, any extra degrad-
ation, rendering components more prone to radiation ef-
fects. It should be noted that the Total IonizingDose (TID)
levels were simulated with the TRAD’s FASTRAD [34]
simulation tool by using the full Computer-aided design
(CAD) of the satellite and the orbital data as input [1]. The
simulated levels for the mission duration were found to be
well within the Radiation Design Margin (RDM) limits.
To increase the reliability and tackle possible issues con-
nected to the STMmicro-controllers (SEE sensitivity), the
team decided to use a radiation-tolerant MCU for the FM
and the equivalent model non-radiation hardened version
for the EQM.
Certain components identified as critical, with miss-
ing radiation-related data, were screened for SEE effects
during the Heavy Ion Testing at UCLouvain.  The main
focus of the testing was on SELs, given their potentially
destructive nature [33].  Before testing the components,
the protective cover was removed through a process called
delidding. With the help of the EEE (Electrical, Electronic
and Electro-mechanical) components lab of ESTEC [35],
part of the casing was eliminated so that the die could be
directly exposed to the Heavy-Ion beam. After the delid-
ding process, the componentswere soldered on a dedicated
testing board.
Fig. 4: Heavy ion testing board with all the components
soldered
Page 5 of 11

The two Environmental Testing Campaigns with a
single EQM model of the AcubeSAT OBC/ADCS board
design serving as the Device Under Test  (DUT) were
conducted at  the ESA CSF, ESEC-Galaxia, Transinne,
Belgium [41]. The campaign consisted of Vibration and
TVAC testing. The detailed Test Specifications and Test
Reports can be found in the relevant Test Specification &
Test Procedures (TSTP) documents [42, 43, 44, 45].
4.1 Tests Configuration
4.1.1 Vibration Testing Description
In accordancewith the "Test as you Fly, Fly as youTest"
testing principle, vibration testing took place before TVAC
tests. During the vibration testing the DUT underwent on
each axis a Resonance Search to identify the natural fre-
quencies of the DUT, followed by a RandomVibration test
and a post Resonance Search to verify structural integrity
by observing minimal frequency and amplitude shifts in
the frequency response. Threemainmeasurement acceler-
ometers were used. Two of themwere placed on theADCS
side, one on top of the MCU where the maximum effects
of the loading are expected, and one near the corner of
the PCB. The third accelerometer was placed on the metal
adaptor used for the mounting on the shaker, along with
two control accelerometers.
4.1.2 TVAC Testing Description
The TVAC testing includes four thermal cycles due to
time constraints. One is non-operational, where the DUT
is turned off, and three are operational where the DUT
must withstand temperature fluctuations while being oper-
ational.  Its functionality is confirmed through functional
IAC–24–E2,4,1,x89777

ess, Milan, Italy, 14-18 October 2023.
ed by the International Astronautical Federation with permission.
tests, either at the maximum temperature point or corres-
pondingly at the minimum.  The placement of the ther-
mocouples is designed to capture the thermal response of
essential components on both the OBC and ADCS sides of
the board and also takes into account the need for adequate
surface area to securely tape down both the thermocouples
and the associated cables.
4.2 Environmental Testing Requirements
The preparation and execution of an environmental
testing campaign for the OBC/ADCS board must adhere
to the system and subsystem requirements outlined in the
Verification Control Document (VCD)s [46, 47]. The test
campaign ensures the board’s capability to function reli-
ably in space conditions by verifying compliance with per-
formance, equipment, and environmental standards. Ad-
ditionally, the testing facilities must meet CubeSat team
standards, such as using appropriate thermal and vibration
testing equipment under controlled conditions. Moreover,
the unique demands of each environmental test—such as
ensuring the board’s functionality is not impacted are veri-
fied through functional tests. The campaign requirements
are specified in the relevant TSTP documents [42, 43, 44,
45].
4.2.1 Vibration Testing Requirements
The vibration testing requirements aim to ensure that
the PCB can endure mechanical stresses without func-
tional degradation, with a particular focus on assessing
its structural integrity and preventing damage. The philo-
sophy also prioritizes careful monitoring through reson-
ant searches and visual inspections to detect any potential
weaknesses. Additionally, precision in the placement and
mounting of measurement tools, such as accelerometers,
is critical to maintaining the reliability and accuracy of the
test results.
4.2.2 Thermal Vacuum Testing Requirements
The TVAC requirements are driven by the philosophy
of replicating the harsh conditions of space to ensure that
the board can perform reliably in extreme thermal and va-
cuum environments. There is an emphasis on maintaining
functional integrity across a wide temperature range and
ensuring that the equipment can operate under stressful
thermal conditions.
4.3 First Campaign
4.3.1 Vibration Testing Results
During all sequences, therewere amplitude shifts in the
pre and post-random Resonance searches. The amplitude
shift on the X axis was attributed to the natural settling
of the DUT, the other shifts were negligible as elaborated

Fig. 5: OBC/ADCS board with all sensor
in [48]. After the tests, the DUT functionality remained
unimpaired. As a result, the testing process was deemed
successful. The pass-fail criteria specified were all met for
the tests conducted and the teamconsiders theOBC/ADCS
board Vibration (VIBE) qualified.
4.3.2 Thermal Vacuum Testing Results
During TVAC testing, after pumping down and before
performing any thermal cycling, it was observed that the
OBCMCUkept resetting at a randomperiod and theMCU
was also informed about Control AreaNetwork (CAN) bus
errors.

This anomaly occurred at approximately 40 ◦C and
functionality recovered after the temperature dropped.
After software debugging at  the CSF [48] during a
high-temperature stimulation with a heat lamp, the cause
of the crash was determined to be the communication
between the MCU and the MRAM along with the over-
clocking of the MCU due to the external 16 MHz Tem-
perature - Compensated Crystal Oscillator (TCXO) that
was outputting a frequency of 304 MHz for the CPU clock
instead of the nominal 300 MHz. This was also causing
the CAN errors.
When the board was subjected to sub-zero temperat-
ures, none of themeasurementswere negative and, instead,
had an offset of around 30 degrees over the chamber’s tem-
perature, indicating an issue in the logic concerning neg-
ative values included in the implementation of the driver.
Furthermore, the ADCS MCU experienced a circumstan-
IAC–24–E2,4,1,x89777

ess, Milan, Italy, 14-18 October 2023.
ed by the International Astronautical Federation with permission.
(b) Board in Thermal Vacuum Chamber
s attached for the VIBE and TVAC tests.
tial crash, occurring at -20 ◦C but was reset by the internal
watchdog and continued to function normally. During the
second cold cycle, the team attempted to underclock the
MCU due to the use of a TCXO crystal that differed from
the one intended for the FM. Although the issue reoc-
curred, the MCU failed to recover and remained in an idle
state.  It is important to note that while the board was in
the TVAC, the team was unable to debug or upload code
due to Ground Support Equipment (GSE) issues, which
limited the ability to troubleshoot in real-time.
Following the TVAC test, all electronics and circuitry
remained operational as verified through the FTs.  The
Product Assurance aspect of TVAC testing is considered
successful and the manufacturing and assembly processes
are considered to be verified.  Nevertheless, functional-
ity in extreme temperatures and vacuum is ultimately not
reliable and the OBC/ADCS board is not TVAC qualified.
4.4 Design Improvements Based on First Campaign
After the first campaign, four Non - Conformance Re-
port (NCR)s were filled, indicating problems with the tem-
perature sensors software [49], MCU crashes at high tem-
peratures [50], communication failures via the CAN Bus
at elevated temperatures [51], and the unresponsiveness
of the ADCS MCU at low temperatures [52].  Each of
these issues was systematically addressed through targeted
design modifications, which were subsequently validated
through rigorous testing.
Page 7 of 11

75th International Astronautical Congr
Copyright ©2024 by Aristotle University of Thessaloniki. Publish
4.4.1 Temperature Sensor Software Malfunctions
The driver caused the external  temperature sensors
to show unreliable temperature values.  More specific-
ally, when the temperature went below 0 ◦C, none of the
measurements were negative and,  instead, had an offset
of around 30 ◦C over the chamber’s temperature. The er-
ror was induced by a fault in the logic concerning negative
values included in the implementation of the driver. Modi-
fications in the driver’s software successfully resolved the
issue as verified by subsequent tests using an MCP9808
[24] evaluation board.
4.4.2 MCU Crashes at High Temperatures
A significant issue identified during testing was the re-
current crashing of the OBCMCU under elevated thermal
conditions.  The analysis in the CSF revealed that  this
instability derived from the use of an Resistor Capacitor
(RC) circuit-based clock, which lacked sufficient thermal
stability, the overclocking of the MCU due to the higher
frequency TCXO and the MRAM’s operations.  To mit-
igate this, the RC circuit-based clock was replaced with a
12 MHz TCXO which offers greater reliability in extreme
temperatures and produces an expected output frequency
to the MCU clock. In addition, the MRAM read and write
timing cycles were optimized via Static Memory Control-
ler (SMC) configuration.
The performance of the system following these changes
improved markedly. The MCU remained operational up
to 49 ◦C, significantly extending its tolerance to high tem-
peratures. Furthermore, isolation heat tests confirmed that
the failure was component-related and originated from the
MCU, as heating the MCU alone caused it to fail at an
internal temperature of 67°C and an ambient of 46 ◦C,
while the MRAM and other components remained stable
for temperatures above 50 ◦C. Subsequent heat tests on an-
other breakout module and anOBCEMboard, both having
similar functionality and architecture, showed stable per-
formance. Upon replacing the defective OBC MCU on
the OBC/ADCS Board, the issue was fully resolved, with
no further crashes observed during the ensuing thermal
testing.
4.4.3 CAN Bus Failures
Errors were occurring on both the OBC and ADCS
during communication over the CAN Bus when the tem-
perature was over 52 ◦C.

In either case, as the temperature
further deviated from ambient, communication was auto-
matically halted as neither side could successfully transmit
or receive messages. The team was forced to use the in-
ternal RC clocks on both MCUs since the TCXO crystal
differed from the one that was intended to be used, due to
supply chain issues at the time of the EQM manufactur-
IAC–24–E2,4,1,x89777

ess, Milan, Italy, 14-18 October 2023.
ed by the International Astronautical Federation with permission.
ing. After replacing the TCXO crystal to match the FM
configuration, the clock output was within the datasheet’s
[53] specification of a 2.5 ppm tolerance anywhere in the
operating range from -40 ◦C to +85 ◦C. the communica-
tion integrity was restored, with the CAN Bus functioning
reliably during high-temperature tests.
4.4.4 ADCS MCU Unresponsiveness at Low Temperat-
ures
At low temperatures, the ADCS MCU became unre-
sponsive, which compromised system functionality during
cold environment tests. While a certain cause has not been
identified yet, the team suspected that the RC circuit-based
clock led to the initial crash during the first cold cycle
and the MCU complete unresponsiveness emerged due
to the underclocking of the microcontroller while using
the wrong model of the temperature-compensated crystal,
during the second cold cycle. However, a failure of the
Central Processing Unit could also occur from the manu-
facturing process resulting in a faulty component. Due to
the high risk of damaging the board with cold tests outside
the TVAC environment, caused by potential condensation
from humidity, no further troubleshooting was conducted.
Given these changes and after thorough testing, having
verified the board’s stable behavior at hot temperatures,
the team decided to repeat the environmental testing to
verify that the solutions were effective.
4.5 Second Campaign
4.5.1 Vibration Testing Results
The OBC/ADCS board encountered significant issues
during the VIBE testing leading to a failure of the quali-
fication process. The testing followed a standard sequence
across all three axes (X, Y, and Z), and while initial results
were in line with expectations, a major anomaly occurred
during the random sequence. A large amplitude shift of
20% was noted, which was attributed to the natural set-
tling of the assembly. However, subsequent tests showed
amplitude shifts reaching 481%, indicating a serious issue
with the integrity of the DUT.
Upon thorough inspection [54] after the Z-axis testing,
it was discovered that two critical inductors, responsible
for magnetometer measurements on the X and Z axes, had
detached from the PCB [55]. This detachment comprom-
ised the data acquisition from themagnetometer, rendering
the results invalid [56]. As a result, the functional tests
post-VIBE were conducted with limited functionality.
4.5.2 Thermal Vacuum Testing Results
The TVAC testing of the DUT encountered a few crit-
ical  issues,  though some components,  like the NAND
Flash, the MRAM, and the CAN Bus of the system per-
Page 8 of 11.

At present, the status of the board indicates that all sys-
tems are operational in ambient conditions and the board
exhibits no critical faults.  Despite that,  the aforemen-
tioned issues related to the MCUs’ unexpected behavior
under certain temperature ranges remain under investig-
ation.  Following the second environmental testing cam-
paign, more extensive testing of the board was required to
identify the issues. The reproduction of the unstable be-
havior was not accomplished during a following thermal
cycle that was conducted on the same board, using the
exact same ground support equipment that was utilized
during the official environmental qualification campaigns.
Regarding the specifications of this test, the temperature
range of 50°C to -30°C, which was considered adequate
for the temperature range that the issues were noticed ini-
tially, was achieved for a shorter duration than the thermal
cycles during the two campaigns, with a lower temperature
gradient and using a thermal chamber, without the effect
of the vacuum conditions.
As of the latest testing phase, after a thorough inspec-
tion of the TVAC Umbilical cable, it was observed that
when bending the cable in different ways the functionality
of the board was affected, resulting in the unstable beha-
vior of the board both in ambient and in testing conditions.
Another significant issue that resulted in the crashes of the
OBCMCU, was identified to be the driver of the MRAM,
which needs to be further optimised to meet critical timing
constraints and edge cases during temperature transitions.
Following the replacement of the umbilical cable to
eliminate any signal degradation, along with necessary
software adjustments,  the OBC MCU demonstrates full
operational capacity under all conditions tested within the
IAC–24–E2,4,1,x89777

ess, Milan, Italy, 14-18 October 2023.
ed by the International Astronautical Federation with permission.
thermal chamber.
Attempts to reproduce the ADCS MCU crash under
similar conditions, outside a TVAC, have so far been un-
successful, leaving it as the sole unresolved issue.

Further investigation is required to reproduce the is-
sues and fully understand the root cause of the MCUs’
unstable performance, under specific environmental con-
ditions. After the identification of the causes, a 3rd Envir-
onmental Testing Campaign will be pursued with a new
manufactured model that will comply with any possible
required changes, induced by the successful resolution of
the problem.
Upon completing the prerequisite Space qualification,
conducted and radiated emission EMC testing will be car-
ried out, avoiding any destructive tests. With the character-
ization of the components of the PCB already completed,
the next step will be the implementation of a Test Plan.
On the firmware side, optimization and finalization
of  the on-board software,  including memory manage-
ment, ECSS services implementation, and subsystem-level
FDIR are included in the required work for the OBC. The
implementation of these is considered necessary to en-
suring the data handling and timing requirements related
to the simultaneous control of the various subsystems of
the satellite, thus minimizing the risk of system conflicts
or communication breakdowns. On the ADCS side, the
pending work comprises the finalization of the actuators’
drivers and the integration of the determination and control
algorithms in the onboard software.
Following the subsystem-level testing and verification,
the integration process will be initiated.  At an early
phase, using a flatsat configuration as described in Acube-
SAT’s Manufacturing Assembly Integration and Verifica-
tion Plan (MAIVP) [16], the communication of the OBC
with the rest of the subsystems of the satellite will be tested
and established. More specifically, the OBC will interface
with the COMMS board, the EPS, the ADCS (including
the ADCS MCU, the Reaction Wheel, the Magnetorquers
and the photodiodes), the Antenna Deployment Mechan-
ism, the Payload and the Solar Panels. Once the integ-
ration is complete hardware-wise, the remaining system-
level software is to be finalized and the implementation
of the switching of the operational modes of the satellite,
FDIR, in orbit patching and packet storage is going to be
tested.

was unsuccessful.
A post-TVAC inspection revealed no visible damage
or deformations to the OBC, ADCS, or the voltage reg-
ulator PCB supplying the EQM. No permanent damage
was sustained, so additional functional tests were deemed
unnecessary.
5. Current Status
At present, the status of the board indicates that all sys-
tems are operational in ambient conditions and the board
exhibits no critical faults.  Despite that,  the aforemen-
tioned issues related to the MCUs’ unexpected behavior
under certain temperature ranges remain under investig-
ation.  Following the second environmental testing cam-
paign, more extensive testing of the board was required to
identify the issues. The reproduction of the unstable be-
havior was not accomplished during a following thermal
cycle that was conducted on the same board, using the
exact same ground support equipment that was utilized
during the official environmental qualification campaigns.
Regarding the specifications of this test, the temperature
range of 50°C to -30°C, which was considered adequate
for the temperature range that the issues were noticed ini-
tially, was achieved for a shorter duration than the thermal
cycles during the two campaigns, with a lower temperature
gradient and using a thermal chamber, without the effect
of the vacuum conditions.
As of the latest testing phase, after a thorough inspec-
tion of the TVAC Umbilical cable, it was observed that
when bending the cable in different ways the functionality
of the board was affected, resulting in the unstable beha-
vior of the board both in ambient and in testing conditions.
Another significant issue that resulted in the crashes of the
OBCMCU, was identified to be the driver of the MRAM,
which needs to be further optimised to meet critical timing
constraints and edge cases during temperature transitions.
Following the replacement of the umbilical cable to
eliminate any signal degradation, along with necessary
software adjustments,  the OBC MCU demonstrates full
operational capacity under all conditions tested within the
IAC–24–E2,4,1,x89777

ess, Milan, Italy, 14-18 October 2023.
ed by the International Astronautical Federation with permission.
thermal chamber.
Attempts to reproduce the ADCS MCU crash under
similar conditions, outside a TVAC, have so far been un-
successful, leaving it as the sole unresolved issue.
6. Future Work
Further investigation is required to reproduce the is-
sues and fully understand the root cause of the MCUs’
unstable performance, under specific environmental con-
ditions. After the identification of the causes, a 3rd Envir-
onmental Testing Campaign will be pursued with a new
manufactured model that will comply with any possible
required changes, induced by the successful resolution of
the problem.
Upon completing the prerequisite Space qualification,
conducted and radiated emission EMC testing will be car-
ried out, avoiding any destructive tests. With the character-
ization of the components of the PCB already completed,
the next step will be the implementation of a Test Plan.
On the firmware side, optimization and finalization
of  the on-board software,  including memory manage-
ment, ECSS services implementation, and subsystem-level
FDIR are included in the required work for the OBC. The
implementation of these is considered necessary to en-
suring the data handling and timing requirements related
to the simultaneous control of the various subsystems of
the satellite, thus minimizing the risk of system conflicts
or communication breakdowns. On the ADCS side, the
pending work comprises the finalization of the actuators’
drivers and the integration of the determination and control
algorithms in the onboard software.
Following the subsystem-level testing and verification,
the integration process will be initiated.  At an early
phase, using a flatsat configuration as described in Acube-
SAT’s Manufacturing Assembly Integration and Verifica-
tion Plan (MAIVP) [16], the communication of the OBC
with the rest of the subsystems of the satellite will be tested
and established. More specifically, the OBC will interface
with the COMMS board, the EPS, the ADCS (including
the ADCS MCU, the Reaction Wheel, the Magnetorquers
and the photodiodes), the Antenna Deployment Mechan-
ism, the Payload and the Solar Panels. Once the integ-
ration is complete hardware-wise, the remaining system-
level software is to be finalized and the implementation
of the switching of the operational modes of the satellite,
FDIR, in orbit patching and packet storage is going to be
tested.
7. Conclusion
The journey of developing AcubeSAT’s OBC/ADCS
board has been one of continuous iterations and learning.
Through extensive testing, the team has refined the design,
